principles.name: Event Must Change a Value
principles.description: Every unit of story (beat, scene, sequence, act) must turn at least one story value from positive to negative or negative to positive. No value change = no event = cut.

principles.name: Change Through Conflict, Never Coincidence
principles.description: Value change must be produced by action meeting a force of antagonism. Coincidence may enter a story but can never resolve it; if used, it must be set up early and pay off meaningfully.

principles.name: The Gap Is the Engine
principles.description: Story is generated in the gap between the protagonist's subjective expectation of his action and the objective result. Widen this gap to deepen meaning; close it too easily and drama collapses.

principles.name: Progression Through Escalation
principles.description: Each event must demand more of the protagonist than the one before — greater risk, greater capacity, stronger antagonism. Lateral or repeated stakes are regression.

principles.name: Irreversibility at the Climax
principles.description: The story climax must deliver absolute, irreversible change; every prior element is engineered to make that final reversal feel both inevitable and surprising.

methods.name: Five-Part Spine
methods.goal: Impose the load-bearing skeleton of any story.
rules: Define an Inciting Incident that visibly upsets the protagonist's life balance, placed within the first 25% of runtime.
From that incident derive a conscious Object of Desire and, when possible, a contradicting unconscious desire.
Build Progressive Complications that raise forces of antagonism after each action.
Force a Crisis: a binary irreducible choice (good vs. good or lesser of two evils) placed immediately before the Climax.
Execute a Climax that delivers absolute, irreversible value change, then a Resolution that releases pressure and shows the new world.

methods.name: Value-Turn Scene Builder
methods.goal: Ensure every scene is a story event, not a transition.
rules: Name the value at stake at scene open and mark its charge (+, -, mixed).
Give the viewpoint character a concrete Scene-Objective tied to the story Spine.
Introduce a force of antagonism that opens a gap between expectation and result.
Turn the value charge by scene close; if unchanged, cut or rewrite.
Locate the Turning Point precisely on the beat the charge flips — not before, not after.
Build the scene out of beats: exchanges of action/reaction behaviour that accumulate toward the turn.

methods.name: Progressive Complication Engine
methods.goal: Escalate by forcing the protagonist past points of no return.
rules: Begin with the protagonist's minimum conservative action that could plausibly succeed.
Answer each action with a reaction stronger or different than expected, opening the gap.
After each reversal, close off the easiest prior option so retreat is impossible.
Engage at least two of three Levels of Conflict (inner, personal, extra-personal); ideally all three.

Drive the final act toward the Negation of the Negation — not the mere opposite of the positive value but a qualitatively worse compound (e.g. justice → unfairness → injustice → tyranny; love → indifference → hate → self-hate masquerading as love).
Never stop at the Contradictory when the Negation of the Negation is reachable.

methods.name: Antagonism Calibration
methods.goal: Make the forces against the protagonist at least equal to, ideally greater than, his capacities.
rules: Inventory every positive value the protagonist holds; build antagonist forces that embody the Contrary, Contradictory, and Negation of the Negation of each.
Never allow the protagonist to succeed through forces weaker than himself.
Distribute antagonism across inner, personal, and extra-personal levels; never resolve all conflict on one plane.
If the protagonist feels too capable for the obstacle, raise the obstacle, not the hero.
If tempted to lower the stakes to preserve outline beats, rewrite the beats instead.

methods.name: Setup / Payoff Ledger
methods.goal: Make every payoff feel inevitable by planting its setup earlier.
rules: For every reveal, reversal, or climactic action, locate or plant at least one prior setup.
Setups must be memorable enough to be retrievable, oblique enough not to telegraph.
Never pay off what was not set up; never set up what is not paid off.
Revise backward from the Climax — trace each climactic element to its earliest necessary plant.
When a plot hole must be closed, prefer a revised setup over a new explanatory scene.

methods.name: Invisible Exposition
methods.goal: Deliver required information without stopping the story.
rules: Dramatize facts as weapons in conflict; never speak information purely for the audience.
Release exposition on a need-to-know basis at the latest possible moment.
Open with curiosity — put a question in the viewer's mind — before supplying backstory.
Choose deliberately per sequence between Mystery (audience knows less than protagonist), Suspense (audience and protagonist know the same), and Dramatic Irony (audience knows more than protagonist).
Test every flashback, dream, montage, and voice-over by removing it: if the story still holds, delete it.
Never let the Inciting Incident of the Central Plot live in backstory — it must happen onscreen.

methods.name: Crisis Dilemma Forge
methods.goal: Make the climactic decision genuinely hard.
rules: Refuse any choice between a clear good and a clear evil — the audience will predict it.
Construct either an irreconcilable good-vs-good or lesser-of-two-evils dilemma.
Ensure both options cost the protagonist something essential he carried into the story.
Place the Crisis immediately before the Climax; do not let time or scenes dilute pressure.
Stage the Crisis in a location and moment that give the decision maximum visibility.

methods.name: Composition and Pacing
methods.goal: Order scenes so tension accumulates and monotony is broken.
rules: Alternate rising action with compressed reflection or relief to avoid emotional monotony.
Escalate the magnitude of each act climax beyond the previous one.
Keep the final act the shortest and fastest; shorten scenes as the Climax approaches.
Vary scene length aggressively — for every short scene a long one, never a flat average.
Make every tier (beat → scene → sequence → act → story) end on a stronger value reversal than the tier below it.
Emotions do not double; once a negative value is reached, the next turn must introduce a new, contrasting charge or a deeper negative, not more of the same.

methods.name: Plot-Shape Selection
methods.goal: Choose consciously among Archplot, Miniplot, and Antiplot and respect the chosen form.
rules: Default to Archplot when the goal is maximal audience engagement: active protagonist, external conflict primary, closed ending, linear time, consistent reality, causality.
Choose Miniplot only by deliberate reduction: passive/internal protagonist, open ending, multi-protagonist structure, or muted closure — and only if the reduced form can still deliver value change.
Choose Antiplot only as conscious revolt: coincidence-driven, nonlinear, inconsistent realities — and commit fully; half-measures read as incompetence.
Never mix reality rules mid-story (e.g., causal for two acts then coincidental) without a signalled break.
Every deviation from Archplot must be a chosen reduction, not a default from weakness.

methods.name: Setting and the War on Cliché
methods.goal: Lock setting hard, then dig deep to generate uncliché material.
rules: Fix four dimensions of setting: period, duration, location, and level of conflict.
Treat setting as a creative limitation: the smaller and more specific, the greater the originality.
Research via memory, imagination, and fact — all three, never only one.
A setting's laws of probability constrain what can and cannot happen; every event must be possible within them.
If an event could occur in many settings it is probably a cliché; force events that can happen only in this setting.

methods.name: Genre Obedience and Reinvention
methods.goal: Satisfy genre conventions before bending them.
rules: Identify primary genre(s) and any subgenres; list their mandatory conventions (settings, roles, events, values).
Deliver every mandatory convention — audiences enter with genre expectations and will feel cheated otherwise.
Reinvent inside the conventions: find a fresh variation on the obligatory scene, do not skip it.
When mixing genres, keep one primary and let others modulate it; avoid equal-weight blends that confuse expectation.
Study the genre's canon deeply enough to recognize its clichés, then refuse them.

methods.name: Controlling Idea Lock
methods.goal: Anchor every structural choice to a single thematic sentence.
rules: State the Controlling Idea as one sentence: Value + Cause (e.g., 'Justice triumphs because the protagonist outwits the corrupt').
Choose consciously whether the charge is Idealist (up-ending), Pessimist (down-ending), or Ironist (both).
Build the Counter-Idea with equal force; the final Climax is the moment the Idea defeats the Counter-Idea.
Reject didacticism: never let a character lecture the Idea; prove it through event.
If a scene does not dramatize either the Idea or the Counter-Idea, it is cargo — cut it.

methods.name: Subplot and Multiplot Discipline
methods.goal: Use secondary plotlines to reinforce, contradict, or complicate the theme.
rules: Every subplot must do one of: (a) contradict the Controlling Idea to create irony, (b) resonate the Idea with variation, (c) complicate the Central Plot by creating obstacles, or (d) set up a Central-Plot Inciting Incident that could not be placed onscreen.
A subplot without one of these functions is cargo — cut it.
Each subplot gets its own Inciting Incident, Progressive Complications, Crisis, Climax, Resolution — though compressed.
When there is no Central Plot (Multiplot), bind the pieces by a single thematic idea visible in every strand.
Never let a subplot climax overshadow the Central Plot climax unless the story is explicitly Multiplot.

methods.name: Inciting Incident Design
methods.goal: Engineer the first major event so it compels everything that follows.
rules: Generate it by one of exactly two means: decision (by protagonist or by someone with power over the protagonist) or coincidence (tragic or serendipitous).
Radically upset the balance of the protagonist's life — for better or for worse.
Arouse a conscious desire (restore balance) and, ideally, a contradictory unconscious desire.
Stage it onscreen for the Central Plot, never in backstory.
Place it as early as the story allows after the minimum exposition needed to make its impact felt.
End it by raising the Major Dramatic Question ('How will this end?') that the Climax will answer.

methods.name: Surprise and Credibility Control
rules: Pursue True Surprise: the revelation of the gap between expectation and result followed by insight, never Cheap Surprise (shock without meaning).
Every surprise must, in retrospect, have been setup — the audience should think 'I should have seen that.'
Diagnose melodrama as under-motivation, never as over-expression; to fix, increase the weight of causes, do not shrink the event.
When logic holes remain, either forge a closing link (via new setup) or accelerate past the hole so the audience lacks time to register it.
Never rely on contrivance: unmotivated convenience that serves the writer's outline but not the characters' stakes.

methods.name: Image System and Symbolic Ascension
methods.goal: Raise the story's symbolic charge without announcing it.
rules: Begin with actions, locations, and objects that represent only themselves.
Return to them, accumulating association, so by late story they carry universal/archetypal weight.
Never label imagery as symbolic in text; the moment the symbol is named, its power dies.
Build two or three image motifs maximum — more dilute each other.
Pair each symbolic motif with a charged value so it gains meaning as values turn.

methods.name: Adaptation Triage
methods.goal: Decide what from the source survives the shift to screen.
rules: Identify where the source's power lives: inner (prose), personal (theatre), or extra-personal (screen) conflict.
If the source's power is primarily inner, externalize it into action or cut it.
Preserve the source's Inciting Incident, Crisis, and Climax; everything else is negotiable.
Collapse characters and subplots aggressively; the screen cannot hold a novel's population.
Betray the source's literal content to preserve its essential Controlling Idea.

methods.name: Progression Patterns
methods.goal: Choose how the story expands from its starting point.
rules: Social Progression: widen the impact of actions outward from a private matter to society.
Personal Progression: drive actions inward into intimate relationships and inner life.
Symbolic Ascension: escalate imagery from particular to universal.
Ironic Ascension: turn progression so result inverts expectation with increasing depth.
Select one dominant pattern; using more than two makes the story diffuse.
Match the chosen pattern to the setting's scale — social for epics, personal for chamber stories.

methods.name: Unity Through Causal Chain
methods.goal: Bind Inciting Incident to Climax so inevitability emerges.
rules: Test: the sentence 'Because of [Inciting Incident], the [Climax] had to happen' must read as logically true.
If that sentence feels forced, the Spine is broken — the story lacks unity.
Every major event between Inciting Incident and Climax must be causally reachable from what precedes it; coincidence may accelerate but never cause the Climax.
Within this causal unity, vary locations, tempos, emotional charges, and levels of conflict as aggressively as possible.

questions: What value is at stake, and what is its charge at the open?
What is the charge at the close? If identical, why does the scene exist?
Where is the Turning Point — the exact beat the charge flips?
What force of antagonism opened the gap between expectation and result?
Is every piece of information delivered here the minimum required, or is there unpaid exposition?
Does the viewpoint character have a Scene-Objective that connects to the Spine?
Do the beats inside the scene escalate in action/reaction, or do they plateau?
Is the Inciting Incident within the first quarter and does it visibly upset life balance?
Can the Spine be named in one sentence of protagonist desire?
Can the Controlling Idea be named in one sentence of value + cause?
Do the act climaxes escalate in magnitude, each stronger than the last?
Does the story reach the Negation of the Negation or stop at mere opposition?
Is the final Climax absolute and irreversible, or could things simply go back?
Has every climactic element been set up earlier in the text?
Does 'Because of [Inciting Incident], the [Climax] had to happen' read as true?
Which plot shape (Archplot, Miniplot, Antiplot) has been chosen, and is it obeyed consistently?
Does any event here resolve by coincidence or by help arriving from outside the story?
Is the protagonist's action the minimum conservative effort appropriate to this stage?
Does the world's reaction exceed or diverge from the protagonist's expectation?
Has a previously-used option been closed off, increasing pressure for the next scene?
Is the Crisis a real dilemma (good vs. good or evil vs. evil), or a rigged choice?
Which Levels of Conflict (inner, personal, extra-personal) are engaged, and is any left unused?
Are the forces of antagonism at least as strong as the protagonist's capacities on every front?
Does every subplot either contradict, resonate, complicate, or enable the Central Plot? If none of these, cut it.
Is the Controlling Idea proved by event, or merely spoken by a character?
Are all genre mandatory conventions present, and are any reinvented rather than skipped?
If this is a surprise moment, is it True Surprise (earned by gap + insight) or Cheap Surprise (shock only)?
If retrospect were possible, could the audience say 'I should have seen that coming'?
Does contrivance resolve anything here — a character conveniently present, a fact conveniently known?
Is the setting locked in period, duration, location, and level of conflict?
Could these events occur in many settings, or only in this one?
Has the research been done across memory, imagination, and fact?
What progression pattern (Social, Personal, Symbolic, Ironic) dominates, and is a second pattern quietly supporting it?

conflicts.resolution: M5 wins: if a setup must be planted to make a later payoff land, plant it even at the cost of slightly more visible exposition — but dramatize the plant per M6.
M1 wins on structural position (the five-part spine is non-negotiable); M4 governs the magnitude of forces within that spine. Never soften antagonism to preserve an outline beat — rewrite the beat.
M3 wins: if escalation demands it, break pacing symmetry. Progression of conflict outranks tidy rhythm.
M7 wins at the story-level Crisis: the climactic scene may violate normal scene-value economy because its job is the final irreversible turn, not a routine value flip.
M9 wins: plot shape is a deeper structural choice than genre. If Miniplot/Antiplot are chosen, some genre conventions may legitimately be muted or broken — but consciously, not by accident.
M12 wins: surprise must never contradict the Controlling Idea. A twist that sells a tonal reversal foreign to the thematic argument is Cheap Surprise regardless of how shocking it feels.
M19 wins: causal unity of the Central Plot is load-bearing. A subplot that strengthens theme but breaks causal reach to the Central Climax must be restructured or cut.
M12 wins: when adapting, betray any literal detail of the source before betraying the Controlling Idea the source earned.

meta.philosophy: Story is the progressive, causally linked change of human values under pressure, driven by the gap between expectation and reality, culminating in an irreversible climax that proves a single controlling idea.
meta.style_bias: classical and dramatic, defaulting to Archplot (closed, causal, linear, consistent, active-protagonist) while remaining capable of deliberate reduction to Miniplot or deliberate revolt via Antiplot; anti-episodic, anti-coincidental, anti-didactic, anti-sentimental, and uncompromising about the equal weight of antagonism and the reach of the Negation of the Negation.
